m00_the_signal.lead_signal: Environmental risks remain the dominant long-term threat across all planetary boundaries. Short-term risk perception has shifted toward geopolitical concerns, but 10-year outlook shows extreme weather, biodiversity loss, and Earth system disruptions as top three risks. Three-quarters of respondents assess environmental outlook as turbulent or stormy.

items.headline: Boundary stack is tightening the strategic operating space
items.summary: The transition from six to seven breached boundaries (via ocean acidification) is a durability signal: mitigation in one domain is increasingly offset by deterioration in others, compressing policy timelines and increasing tail-risk coupling between climate, food systems, and maritime security.

items.headline: IPCC AR7 cycle governance sets the next decision window
items.summary: IPCC-64 (24–27 March 2026) advanced AR7 work programme and upcoming deliverables (cities report in 2027; CDR/CCUS methodology in 2027). This shapes the global evidence base and, indirectly, regulatory baselines that drive capital allocation and litigation risk over 2026–2029.

m02_planetary_boundaries.week_development: WMO State of Global Climate 2025 report confirms planet entering state of energy imbalance. Ocean absorbing vast majority of excess heat, accelerating sea-level rise and committing Earth to centuries of planetary shift.

key_judgments.judgment: AMOC tipping trajectory has escalated to the dominant Earth system signal of the current reporting cycle, with multiple converging Tier 2 studies identifying new early-warning indicators and quantifying previously underexplored carbon cycle feedback loops
El Niño development confirmed by C3S Tier 1 data opens bleaching window for coral reefs and elevates drought precursor risk in Central America Dry Corridor, Southeast Asia, and northern South America

AMOC collapse carbon cycle feedback of 47 to 83 ppm additional atmospheric CO2 creates a positive feedback loop between tipping system and climate change planetary boundary, materially altering long-run carbon pricing and stranded asset calculations

weekly_brief:
## Lead Signal

Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation risk intensified this cycle as multiple Tier 2 studies converged on new early warning indicators and quantified carbon cycle feedbacks. A 2026 Communications Earth and Environment paper identified a Gulf Stream abrupt northward displacement of 219 km within two years near Cape Hatteras as an early warning indicator that appears decades before simulated Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation collapse. Satellite altimetry data from 1993 to 2024 and subsurface temperature observations from 1965 to 2024 confirm a statistically significant northward Gulf Stream trend at Cape Hatteras, providing observational grounding for this precursor signal. Within the tipping point tracker, Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation is now characterised as approaching with high preliminary probability, with observational constraints projecting approximately 50 percent weakening by 2100 under a medium emissions scenario that is around 60 percent stronger than model only estimates.

The planetary boundary framing emphasises how these Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation dynamics interact with the climate change boundary. The climate change boundary remains beyond high risk, with the Copernicus April 2026 bulletin confirming the second highest extra polar sea surface temperatures on record and a strengthening El Nino signal in which more than 50 percent of ensemble members exceed 2.5 degrees C amplitude in the Nino3.4 index. A separate Communications Earth and Environment study quantifies an Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation collapse carbon cycle feedback of 47 to 83 ppm additional atmospheric CO2, adding approximately 0.2 degrees C global warming at higher background concentrations. This feedback creates a positive loop between the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation tipping element and the climate change planetary boundary, materially altering long run physical risk envelopes for fossil and coastal assets and reinforcing the current assessment that Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation tipping is the dominant Earth system signal in this reporting cycle.

## Other Developments

**Planetary boundaries status and El Nino linked stressors** The planetary status snapshot records six planetary boundaries exceeded, one at high risk, two in increasing risk, and none in the safe zone, with the aggregate status delta assessed as stable over the week. Within this frame, the climate change boundary remains beyond high risk, anchored in the Copernicus April 2026 bulletin that reports global mean surface temperature anomalies 0.52 degrees C above the 1991 to 2020 average for April 2026 and second highest extra polar sea surface temperatures on record. El Nino development continues to strengthen, with more than 50 percent of ensemble members exceeding 2.5 degrees C amplitude in the Nino3.4 index in the latest seasonal forecast. This El Nino trajectory acts as a cross cutting stressor for biosphere integrity, land system change, freshwater use, and ocean acidification, opening a bleaching window for coral reefs and elevating drought precursors in Central America, Southeast Asia, and northern South America.

**Biosphere integrity and Amazon dieback precursor signals** Biosphere integrity remains beyond high risk, with the Living Planet Index baseline continuing to frame long run biodiversity loss. This cycle did not add new quantified loss data but El Nino development elevates stress on tropical forest biomes and strengthens drought precursor signals relevant to Amazon dieback. In the tipping point tracker, Amazon dieback is assessed as approaching with medium preliminary probability, with an El Nino drought forecast for northern South America identified as the key indicator. The cascade chain links drier conditions to elevated drought, increased fire risk in Amazon transition zones, and downstream food stress and displacement in the Venezuela and Colombia border region, although no new fire or deforestation metrics from national monitoring agencies were added this cycle. The gaps register notes that Inpe Amazon fire data or deforestation metrics would be required to upgrade trajectory assessment confidence.

**Coral reef collapse, aerosol pathways, and attribution gaps** Coral reef collapse is assessed as imminent with high preliminary probability, driven by combined sea surface temperature anomalies and regulatory driven aerosol changes. The tipping point tracker highlights C3S El Nino development and the second warmest March sea surface temperatures as opening a bleaching window, while a Communications Earth and Environment study finds that IMO 2020 ship sulfur cuts reduced aerosol cooling over the Great Barrier Reef, adding 11 watts per square meter radiation and increasing thermal stress by 5 to 10 percent. This aerosol pathway is mirrored in the atmospheric aerosols and novel entities planetary boundaries, where IMO 2020 sulfur cuts are described as reducing aerosol cooling and thereby increasing Great Barrier Reef thermal stress by 5 to 10 percent via a novel pathway, creating unintended ecological consequences while contributing to a wider synthetic chemical and pollutant burden. The attribution gap cases catalogue this as an atmospheric aerosol loading case with no binding framework, and a coral reef bleaching case where there is no international liability framework despite a 5 to 10 percent increase in Great Barrier Reef thermal stress from ship sulfur cuts. The aerosol dual risk flag for the Great Barrier Reef reinforces that 11 watts per square meter added radiation and associated 5 to 10 percent thermal stress increase arise from a regulatory decision that reduces aerosols but imposes regional ecological cost.

**Freshwater, land, and climate security cascades under El Nino** Freshwater use and atmospheric aerosols remain in increasing risk status with stable deltas, while land system change, ocean acidification, and novel entities remain beyond high risk with stable deltas. Within this configuration, El Nino functions as a key organising driver for socio ecological cascades. In the climate security nexus, the Central America Dry Corridor faces severe food stress risk as El Nino drought through boreal summer 2026 is forecast to reduce rainfall, drive crop failure, and escalate food insecurity that in turn drives internal displacement and northward migration pressure, with elevated political instability risk. Southeast Asia is assessed at moderate water stress risk as El Nino drier conditions reduce water availability, disrupt agriculture, and increase transboundary resource tensions in the Mekong basin. Northern South America carries moderate food stress risk as El Nino drought forecasts link to Amazon drought precursors and displacement and instability in the Venezuela and Colombia border region. These regional cascade chains are all classified as active at the physical trigger stage, reflecting that climate signal intensification is already feeding into food and water security pressures even in the absence of new conflict events.

**Governance and loss and damage signals** Environmental governance signals remained structurally weak this cycle. The environmental governance composite score is recorded at zero with a stable direction, with component scores for regulatory coverage, enforcement capacity, scientific readiness, mitigation alignment, and international coordination all at zero and no new governance developments reported. In the loss and damage tracker, the mechanism is operationalised but total committed and disbursed funding remain at zero, implying a disbursement ratio of zero and no new commitments or disbursements this cycle. International legal processes show similar stasis: the International Court of Justice climate advisory opinion proceedings remain pending with no material updates this week and no new milestone dates recorded. Attribution gap cases beyond aerosols and coral reefs, including synthetic chemical releases with more than 140,000 synthetic chemicals and fewer than 3,000 tested for toxicity, and deep sea mining under a contested International Seabed Authority governance framework, have no new developments despite their structural significance for the novel entities boundary.

## Cross Monitor Connections

The Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation signal and El Nino linked cascades create several explicit cross monitor linkages that are already reflected in other analytical streams. For the conflict escalation monitor, the Central America Dry Corridor case connects a physical El Nino drought forecast through boreal summer 2026 with crop failure, severe food insecurity, internal displacement, and increased northward migration pressure, alongside elevated political instability risk. Similar chains in Southeast Asia and northern South America, where water and food stress risks are assessed as moderate, feed directly into transboundary resource tensions in the Mekong basin and instability risks in the Venezuela and Colombia border region. These cascades provide a climate conflict nexus that the conflict escalation monitor can ingest as precursor signals for potential political violence and governance stress.

For the macro monitor, Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation carbon cycle feedback and El Nino driven production risks are salient. The assessment that Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation collapse would release 47 to 83 ppm additional atmospheric CO2 and add approximately 0.2 degrees C global warming, combined with projections of approximately 50 percent weakening by 2100 under observational constraints, materially alters long run physical risk pricing for fossil and coastal assets. This is already surfaced as a green finance signal that the macro monitor can use when evaluating stranded asset risk, sovereign climate vulnerability, and long horizon discounting. El Nino forecast risks to rice, coffee, and palm oil output in Southeast Asia and Central America, implied by regional drought and water stress assessments, provide a resource security channel to commodity price volatility and terms of trade shocks that sit within the macro monitor remit.

Information operations and democratic integrity monitors are also implicated. The interpreter identifies that a projected 1.5 degrees C breach by 2034, along with high salience Copernicus 1.5 degrees C exceedance signals and Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation findings, are likely to become targets for climate focused foreign information manipulation and interference. For the democratic integrity monitor, this maps to climate commitments and nationally determined contributions structural insufficiency that can affect domestic legitimacy of Paris Agreement ratifiers. For the foreign information manipulation and interference and cognitive warfare monitor, the same high salience scientific findings about El Nino, Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation weakening, and coral reef collapse offer anchor points for disinformation campaigns that either exaggerate collapse risks to erode trust in institutions or understate them to delay mitigation.

## Outlook

Near term monitoring will focus on whether the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation precursor indicators and El Nino trajectory continue to strengthen. The gaps register highlights that freshwater flux readings from the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation Field of View of Systems array at 34 degrees south would allow an upgrade of Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation proximity assessment from assessed to high confidence, and that Inpe Amazon fire and deforestation data would similarly refine Amazon dieback assessments. In water coral bleaching confirmation from NOAA Coral Reef Watch or the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park Authority would allow an upgrade of coral reef collapse proximity from high to confirmed confidence, closing an important observational gap in the current tipping point analysis.

On the socio ecological side, the active cascade chains in the Central America Dry Corridor, Southeast Asia, and northern South America will remain key watchpoints for conflict escalation, migration, and governance stress in the coming months. The absence of progress in loss and damage financing, zero environmental governance composite scores, and unresolved attribution gaps for aerosols, synthetic chemicals, coral reef bleaching, and deep sea mining mean that escalation in physical climate and tipping point signals is not being matched by governance response. Unless forthcoming cycles record movement on these governance and finance fronts, the structural imbalance between accelerating Earth system risks and institutional capacity to manage them will persist, amplifying the implications of any further shifts in Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation, El Nino, or associated tipping systems.

flags.headline: Approximately 40 percent of intrastate conflicts over recent decades linked to natural resource pressures. Environmental stress exacerbates economic risks and acts as driver of social instability. Food stress, water stress, and resource competition create conflict precursors in climate-vulnerable regions.

Economic losses from weather and climate extremes now approximately 500 billion USD in Europe alone, with much damage remaining uninsured. Creates financial stability risk, government fiscal stress, and stranded asset exposure in climate-vulnerable regions.

Environmental governance gaps and regulatory vacuums create space for political capture and austerity-driven policy reversals. Climate policy suppression and environmental activist repression indicate civic space constraints in climate-vulnerable states.

open_findings.why_it_matters: Materially upgrades risk envelope for AMOC-driven climate disruption beyond IPCC AR6 model ensembles

open_findings.headline: AMOC collapse modelled to release 47 to 83 ppm additional atmospheric CO2 and add approximately 0.2 degrees C global warming
open_findings.why_it_matters: Establishes previously underexplored positive feedback loop between AMOC tipping and climate change planetary boundary

open_findings.headline: C3S April 2026 bulletin confirms second-highest extra-polar SSTs on record as El Niño development strengthens
open_findings.why_it_matters: Opens bleaching window for coral reefs and elevates drought precursor risk in multiple regions

open_findings.headline: IMO 2020 sulfur cuts reduced aerosol cooling over Great Barrier Reef, adding 11 watts per square meter radiation and increasing thermal stress by 5 to 10 percent
open_findings.why_it_matters: Identifies novel pathway by which ship sulfur regulation unintentionally elevates coral bleaching risk
